title: Part 23
text: decent economic theory, especially any decent political economy, must take its departure from the in-between of negotiated value, and from the in-betweenness of human Liminality and the Modern 222 passions. The volatile ups and downs of current economics cannot be reduced to shortcomings in the institutional setting up of market forces; they are examples of imitative behaviour that is driven by passion and emotion – greed in the bubbles, fear in the crashes. Individual investors magnetically join the crowd of others in a rush to get in or out of the market. Human beings are imitative creatures; this is even more the case once we live under the illusion that only children and animals imitate, whereas mature adults – in the best of Kantian traditions – exercise autonomous reason. Human beings, adults included, do imitate. In the contemporary situation, with processes of imitation spreading throughout the social body and on a global scale, connected to the viral networks of the internet, we can no longer afford to forget about Tarde. Imitation does not have to pass through one’s consciousness; in actual fact, Tarde argued, it happens even more smoothly when people do not recognize the fact that they are actually imitating somebody else (Tarde 1903: 77). The somnambulist follows ideas suggested by others, but believes that these are spontaneous and his own (ibid.). The possibility of illusion only increases with what Tarde calls the rise of civilization and increasing urban density – and which we can in many ways translate to our current ‘globalization’. This is because civilization (globalization) diminishes distance between people and increases the frequency of their interaction, thus renders them even more prone to imitation. A particularly clear example of such illusion concerns again the (neoclassical) economic axiom that human beings exert ‘rational choice’. However, in actual fact it is quite evident that the smooth activity of the market is also secured by the media and advertising, working through images and imitation. The short-sightedness of modern economic theory in this regard is truly astonishing, and Tarde is absolutely right in inserting a perplexed question in brackets: ‘How was it possible, then, for economists to dream of formulating theories of value in which the idea of imitation had no part?’ (ibid.: 16). Home and Dwelling O how amiable are thy dwellings, thou Lord of hosts! (Book of Psalms, 84) From an anthropological and sociological perspective, the most intriguing (and frightening) aspect of the history of economic bubbles is the fact that the current bubble-produced crisis emanated from within the most intimate and sacred place we know of: our homes. We need to understand this extremely well: the deregulation and generalized de-framing that took place from the neoliberal triumph of the 1990s very quickly targeted for speculation the most ‘real’ and substantial aspect of humanity: our dwelling practices. The housing bubble which brought the global economy to its knees was anticipated by The Economist in an article with the title Worldwide rise in house prices is the biggest bubble in history. The economic crisis that hit the world from 2008 emanated from speculation on the By Way of Conclusion: Out of Liminality 223 value of people’s homes and houses. This amounts to nothing less than liminality erupting from within the core of primary sociability. From 2008, the housing market entered a crisis in many parts of the world. We need to be extremely careful again here, and get behind the understanding of the word ‘crisis’. From the prevailing economic rationality, the crisis is connected to the fact that prices go down, and that the market is ‘stuck’. The fact that enormous numbers of people and families have literally lost their homes should and must not be neglected, but in economic terms, this is not exactly where the nature of the crisis resides. When the market is stuck, it means that fewer people are selling and fewer people are buying. In other words, people stay where they are, and keep living in their houses, and less money is generated. The very fact that this is automatically seen as a crisis is the real crisis, a clear indication that contemporary culture is problematically oikophobic at its core. But this is the rationality, and governments constantly run behind it as they desperately try to introduce reforms that will inject new energy into the housing market. The Girardian mimetics of desire has taken as its object the house and thrown the realness of real estate into the thin air of the unreal. Some other seeming trivialities start to assume their full significance when seen in this light. During the last two decades, all major dailies in most of the Western world (but not only) increasingly started to include inserts on ‘Housing’ or ‘Home’. They quickly grew in size and in many cases became a whole paper within the paper. 3 They are always inserted at weekends, when newspapers lie around the home and when we have time to read them; when we have time to fantasize; when we are, in fact, dwelling. The inserts are free, as their production costs are financed by the real estate business. Very often, the commercial adverts for houses for sale are preceded by a series of articles, full of images, on architecture, design and ‘living’, fancy new objects which we did not know that we needed until we saw them pictured. It is increasingly unclear whether the articles are written by sales persons or journalists specialized in fashion and living – and sometimes they are actually written as a joint production of the two. Typically, in such inserts, a ‘house of the week’ is presented, with selected images and an interview with the owner. Just as people dreamed of becoming ‘worker of the month’ under communism or in American-style corporate business, we can now dream of creating the perfect house of the week and make it to the news. The real estate market thrives on the unreal, or rather, it thrives on eliminating the very boundary of the real and unreal. What’s in a Name: Home Again At the more anthropological level of dwelling practices, one can observe another phenomenon which surely relates to the increasing speed with which 3 Evidently enough, the instigation of desire for a better and bigger and cooler house equally works through the internet, where we can click around and have a look at houses for sale. All larger newspapers now have housing market search engines on their websites. Liminality and the Modern 224 we change dwellings. Up until quite recently, houses in many parts of Europe were given a name, a fact well-documented by folklorists. As Simmel noted, ‘The house that is called by its own name [gives] its inhabitants a feeling of spatial individuality, of belonging to a qualitatively fixed point in space. Through the name associated with it, the house forms a much more autonomous, individually nuanced existence’ (Simmel quoted in Keohane 2013: 65). One is reminded of one of Bourdieu’s best writings, namely his structural analysis of the Kabyle house where he shows how the house serves as a spatial-semantic ordering of a moral universe (Bourdieu 1970). This is not particular to the Kabyle: in most cultural settings we know off, the house is imbued with meaningfulness in each of its material manifestations. In Denmark, where he who writes grew up, one can still see these names on houses, especially in towns and villages. Trivial as such names are, they testify to an understanding of a house as having a soul and identity, and often also a history connected to that of a family. The specific names used for houses often also refer to a value. In Denmark, most of the names given to houses during the nineteenth century belonged to one of the following six categories (Porsmose 2008: 152): 1. Names indicating hope connected to fertility (Heldbo, house of luck). 2. Names indicating a particular civic virtue (Nøjsomhed, humbleness). 3. Names signalling a reward or a wish for a good, quiet life (Fredenshjem, home of peace). 4. Names indicating satisfaction (Lykkebo, life in happiness). 5. Patriotic names (Danebo, house of Danes). 6. Names indicating a beautiful location (Skøndal, beautiful valley or Stella Maris, star of the seas). In most European locations, house-naming anticipated by centuries the naming and numbering of streets, indicating the centrality of houses in spatial and semantic worldmaking. It is no coincidence that royal families in Europe recognized and bespoke themselves as ‘houses’: the house was the primary spatial substance from where the family genealogy could unfold in history. The ‘dynasty’ referred to the power of the ruler (from the Greek, dynasthai, to ‘have power’), but the self- identification of the royal family remained tied to the notion of a house. Such house-naming practices have a deep and important history. In many parts of the world, newly built houses are baptized just like persons in a ritual celebration. In Rites of Passage Arnold van Gennep starts his empirical discussion of liminality and ritual passages with a careful consideration of such practices, as ‘the sacrifices associated with laying the foundation for a house and constructing a house fall into the category of rites of passage’ (1960: 23). Ceremonies connected to the first dwelling of a house include rites of separation, the ritual lifting of a taboo, and rites of incorporation: a ceremonial visiting, a consecration of the various parts of the building (in many Catholic countries this is still today done by the local priest who sprinkles the walls with holy water while reciting from By Way of Conclusion: Out of Liminality 225 the Bible), and often the sharing of salt and bread, or any other symbolic meal. These ceremonies, notes van Gennep, ‘are essentially rites identifying the future inhabitants with their new residence’ (ibid.: 24). In many cases, this ritual is seen as the last ritual of reintegration closing the ritual sequence of the marriage ceremony; the widespread European tradition that the groom has to carry the bride over the threshold of the front door resumes its full significance within this ceremonial whole. We cannot possibly underestimate the significance of all this; in fact, as van Gennep opens his next chapter, Individuals and Groups, ‘A society is similar to a house divided into rooms and corridors’ (van Gennep 1960: 26). Possibly going beyond what van Gennep meant to imply here, this is because any meaningful formation of public life must flow from the oikos, from how we dwell. This recognition implies something quite different from the ‘protection’ of our private and public spheres of life from market mechanisms and state power, as Habermas wants it; it is rather the recognition, formulated with such great clarity by Aristotle, and Plato before him, that the order of society must rest upon the order of the soul. In Ancient Greece, as Simmel reminds us, to sell a family home or a landed property was ‘an offence not only against the children, but also against the ancestors, because it disrupted the continuity of the family’ (Simmel 2011: 259; see again Keohane 2013).The house and its fence protected man against enemies and other dangers. Its divine protector was Zeus. The Greek word for fence was herkos, and herkeios is an epithet of Zeus. According to Homer, the altar of Zeus Herkeios generally stood in the courtyard before the house, where sacrifices and libations were offered to him. Even more important and interesting is another form of Zeus in which he appears as a ‘house god’, Zeus Ktesios. This is not much discussed by ancient historians, maybe because the simple house cult belonged to those daily routines that quite often do not find their way into our written records. The hearth was the centre of the house cult and of the piety of daily life (see Nilsson 1940). The Greek word denoting the larger household was of course oikos. 4 As van Gennep was well-aware, such rites connected to dwelling cannot be catalogued as superstition: they are distillations of centuries, indeed millennia, of human experience, realizing over and again, and with the same awe, the sacrality of dwelling and the irreducible importance of establishing and maintaining human relations within some form of recognized boundaries. When such a recognition of meaningful boundaries is torn apart and thrown into oblivion, much is at risk. Home and Feeling-at-Home as Theoretical Premise The pressure toward constant transformation which is everywhere today, is intimately related to a sense of exile and homelessness which is also found at 4 Weber discussed this concept in several places, most explicitly in Chapter IV of Economy and Society, entitled Household, Enterprise and Oikos (Weber 1978: 370–84); perhaps too reductively, he saw the oikos as ‘organized want satisfaction’ (ibid.: 381). Liminality and the Modern 226 the level of thought and philosophy. Any effective response to this development must entail a reconsideration of human experiences of liminality and how such experiences can and must be channelled back into a feeling-at-home in the world. It is impossible simply to feel at home everywhere if one does not feel at home somewhere. The recognition of the liminal characteristics of modernity was certainly what brought Szakolczai in the ‘opposite’ direction, leading him to introduce the concept of ‘home’ as central to social and political theory (Szakolczai 2008). It does in fact seem that there is no other remedy to permanent liminality than to re-establish some kind of background in which individual action can be understood and measured, and in which frenetic movement finds a rest. More than ever, we need to turn to the concreteness of lived space. This discussion takes us right back to the attempts by Victor Turner to align his study of ritual passages with an experientially-based philosophy, via Dilthey. What Dilthey objected to was not only the ‘objectifying’ tendencies of modern philosophy, but also something existing at a deeper level, namely the positing of an external position from where the thinker could engage with and order the universe. We have already argued that such an external position was what unified the world- views of Descartes and Hobbes, and was brought to its climax in Kant. Dilthey argued that such an external perspective, reminiscent of the natural sciences, would only create a splintered image of the human being (Szakolczai 2011). Dilthey’s philosophy of life instead takes as its starting point life itself as it comes to consciousness in the individual in the act of living experience (Erlebnis). However, to Dilthey, as to Kierkegaard, this living experience can never be a solipsistic experience. The self is always embedded in transpersonal relations with objects and other subjects. Dilthey would always insist on the concreteness of these relations. The individual, in the midst of these relations, tries to cognize the world in which he lives with a Weltbild. As Turner understood perfectly well, such a Weltbild is not just a cognitive phenomenon: it draws on feeling, willing and thinking, on the various conative, cognitive and affective dimensions, in proportions that vary, but whose bringing together in lived reality has clearly aesthetic qualities. Once again, this was in fact something that Arnold van Gennep had more or less taken for granted. To him, the study of society meant ‘to show the interaction of the feelings, concepts and judgments that constitute individual psychic activity and collective psychic activity within a given social group’ (as quoted in Belmont 1979: 130). As he jotted down in his unpublished notes, any custom ‘is the expression of a psychic, rational, emotional and social complex’ (ibid.: 131). And to van Gennep, no custom existed on its own: it was organically linked to all others. The notion of Weltbild and Dilthey’s attempt to get to the core of experience is therefore also part of Dilthey’s effort to overcome the dualism between object and subject. He wanted to ground a philosophy which could deal with life as lived by real human beings, one that is therefore also concerned with wholeness, not fragmentary, disconnected individual experience. Cartesian self-introspection By Way of Conclusion: Out of Liminality 227 would in and by itself lead nowhere. In Szakolczai’s reconstruction, Dilthey’s great intuition, which he never managed to fully substantiate, was that: this implies starting in the middle, instead of dividing and separating everything from the start into irreconcilable dualisms and dichotomies. Erlebnis, Weltanschauung and Weltbild each belong to this in-between, at the same time foundational and formative space. Weltanschauung and Weltbild, in particular, are aspects of those conceptual tools by which human beings try to make sense of their experiences, interpreting the events around them, giving and discovering meaning in their lives. (Szakolczai 2011: 107) Following this line of reasoning also means facing two alternative starting points for living and thinking: either being attuned to the world or being split from it, either living the world as a cosmos or living the world as a chaos, either reasoning from within the bounds of the world and its inbuilt rhythms, or forcing an accommodation of oneself to an external, alien reality. A Sort of Homecoming: On Being-at-Home in a Meaningful World We live in this world. There is no other world, and hence no possibility of withdrawal. Any attempts to ‘take us out of modernity’ are tragically part of the modern project. As Foucault spelled out very clearly in his last writing (Foucault 1984), this was precisely Kant’s definition of the Enlightenment: an exit. Any position of exteriority is futile. Any imagined position of a-posteriority is merely tragic, part of the utopian drive that gave the modern project much of its destructive energy. Such positions are not far away from the indeed untenable positions of scientific objectivity that dominated most of the twentieth century. They are all predicated upon a sense of alienation, a setting oneself against or above given conditions; they imply the loss of that balance between ‘involvement and detachment’ (Elias) which is a pre-condition of effective thought and practice. This book has attempted to open a debate on how we think and live with liminality today. The clear tendency in the reception and application of liminality in the social and cultural sciences, in art and performance, in business and consultancy, is that liminality represents an unordered, chaotic element of creativity and freedom in a modern world that was drowning with too much rationality. Liminality is adopted as fitting within a larger framework of social constructivism, or as resonating with post-structuralist theories, positing themselves against more structural or nominalist philosophies or world-views. As we have argued throughout, there are compelling reasons to reject this reception altogether, and in a most fundamental way. Social constructivism developed as a paradigm in order to stress human agency in the meaningful construction of the ‘world’ as we see and live and act within it. As a paradigm, it presented itself as an alternative and correction to more dominant rationalist-positivist scientific views, from rational choice theory in political science to realism in Liminality and the Modern 228 international relations. And yet rational choice theory and social constructivism share a foundational epistemology: namely that the world itself is essentially unordered, and that human beings impose their order upon it via their (rational or not, individual or social) choices, ideas and acts. Rationalism and social constructivism alike are predicated upon a prior distancing to the world. They merely perpetuate a kind of epistemic homelessness which goes to the heart of modern thinking. This is certainly not where Arnold van Gennep belongs. For van Gennep the basic fact of transition somehow tied human beings and ‘culture’ to nature. Transitions from group to group or from one social situation to the next are a ‘fact of society’, van Gennep said (1960: 3). However, for van Gennep transitions are also a ‘fact of nature’, and in a sense it was this view he made explicit in Rites of Passage. As he introduces the notion of transitions, he claims that ‘[t]he universe itself is governed by a periodicity which has repercussions on human life, with stages and transitions, movements forward, and periods of relative inactivity’ (ibid.). Van Gennep somehow bases his entire world-view on a certain degree of homology or at least coincidence between social and natural rhythms. It is also this vision which brings him into close proximity with Tarde’s sociology. The world may seem chaotic, but in actual fact, Tarde says, the social world, just like nature, is dominated by regularities. The task of science is not to impose some order on phenomena from the outside through abstract, mental, cognitive operations, but rather to recognize the character and principles of this order. In doing so it is helped by the fact that the nature of this order is fundamentally identical in the diverse orders of reality: it is based on regularity and repetition. In the inorganic world repetitiveness is based on periodic and vibratory movements like waves; in living beings on heredity and genetics, while in social life on imitation (see Tarde 1903, Chapter 1). These repetitive regularities are the basis of both order and change. The various orders of reality are built one upon the other, and do not simply replace and cancel each other. In one of the unfinished notes he made for what was intended to the general conclusion to his multi-volume work, Manual of French Folklore, van Gennep explains that the system of classification that he adopted is ‘rhythmic’ (see Belmont 1979: 98). Ritual stages play such a fundamental role in social life ‘because life itself progresses by oscillations and by stages’ (ibid.). This view should easily be seen as a mature statement relating back to what van Gennep – when he was still engaged with sociology and anthropology – had tried to establish as a ‘life science’, studying ‘living facts’. The human being, from such a view, had to be studied as a part of nature. This was never meant as a reductionism, as we see so often in the recurrent attempts to explain human and social phenomena with reference to ‘nature’ or ‘biology’; almost quite the contrary: it comes much closer to an appreciation of forms, those ‘patterns that connect’, and which Bateson always defined as his real objects of study. The recognition of such rhythms, and even their re-presentation in textual form was for van Gennep tied to an aesthetic By Way of Conclusion: Out of Liminality 229 dimension, which he indicated with surprising explicitness in another of his notes, left-behind and picked up by Belmont, which is worth quoting at length: A folklorist, unless he is a pure theoretician and has never undertaken investigation in the field, is necessarily sensitive to lines (forms) and colours, to music and sounds, to others’ psychic shifts and changing expressions of them. What one might call professional contact with vital rhythms reacts on the rhythm of one’s own writing; sympathy with the living is unconsciously expressed in the contouring of one’s sentences ... (ibid.: 100) Van Gennep returned to this underlying vision in the final pages of Rites de Passage, ending on the beautiful and significant closure of the book: Finally, the series of human transitions has, among some peoples, been linked to the celestial passages, the revolutions of the planets, and the phases of the moon. It is indeed a cosmic conception that relates the stages of human existence to those of plant and animal life and, by a sort of pre-scientific divination, joins them to the great rhythms of the universe. (van Gennep 1960: 194) 5 The style and content of this closing paragraph closely resembles the cosmology found in Plato’s Timeus, the work in which Plato most clearly posits the recognition of the beauty and order of the natural world as the condition for living and thinking rationally. Nature must here be understood in the best of classical traditions as a meaningfully ordered and animated universe, rather than a chaos upon which we, human beings, must impose our order. In normative terms, this can also be said differently: the role of human beings in the universe is not to erect order, create schemes, concepts and models and then impose these upon an unstructured chaos, to ‘build the world’ from scratch. This tendency was what Eric Voegelin recognized as the gnostic drive of modernity. Instead, our role in this universe and on this planet – the only one we have – should rather be to humbly ‘tune in’ to the beauty of the world. Or, as van Gennep put it, join the great rhythms of the Universe. 5 In his hand-written notes to the first edition of his book, included in the French 1969 edition, van Gennep marked out that he should have capitalized the last word to read ‘Universe’.
